items.q: What is the difference between just in time and just in case production?
items.correct: JIT orders materials to arrive only when needed, cutting storage costs; JIC deliberately holds buffer stock to cope with surprises in supply or demand.
items.misconception: JIT and JIC are the same system described from two viewpoints.
items.random_wrong: JIT means products are made one at a time by hand.
items.why: The similar names cause swapping. Remember the trade-off: JIT saves storage but is vulnerable to supply disruption; JIC is resilient but ties up money in stock.

items.q: Does lean production simply mean employing fewer workers?
items.correct: No - lean production means systematically eliminating all waste, including overproduction, waiting, defects and excess transport, while preserving customer value.
items.misconception: Yes - lean means running the factory with the minimum number of staff.
items.random_wrong: Yes - lean refers to making products lighter in weight.
items.why: Students fixate on cost-cutting. Lean targets wasteful activity of every kind; a lean plant may employ the same people but redeploy them to value-adding work.

items.q: If a factory has quality control inspectors, does it automatically have quality assurance?
items.correct: No - QC inspection only catches defects after they occur; QA is a wider system that designs processes so defects are prevented in the first place.
items.misconception: Yes - quality control and quality assurance are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Yes - QA is just the modern name for QC.
items.why: Inspection alone is reactive. QA includes training, process design, standards and documentation across the whole organisation, with QC as just one component.

items.q: Which best describes just in time (JIT) production?
items.correct: Stock arrives just before it is needed, reducing storage costs
items.misconception: Large buffer stocks are held in warehouses
items.random_wrong: Products are only made one at a time
items.why: Correct answer: Stock arrives just before it is needed, reducing storage costs. “Large buffer stocks are held in warehouses” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The main aim of lean production is to:
items.correct: Eliminate waste while maintaining value for the customer
items.misconception: Maximise the size of the workforce
items.random_wrong: Increase the amount of stock held
items.why: It's Eliminate waste while maintaining value for the customer. “Maximise the size of the workforce” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the key difference between quality control and quality assurance?
items.correct: QC inspects outputs for defects; QA builds quality into the whole process to prevent defects
items.random_wrong: QC happens before production, QA afterwards
items.why: Correct answer: QC inspects outputs for defects; QA builds quality into the whole process to prevent defects. “They are identical processes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Computers link and control design, production, handling and quality as one system
items.misconception: Designers email drawings to the factory
items.why: Correct answer: Computers link and control design, production, handling and quality as one system. “Designers email drawings to the factory” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Holding just-in-case stock costs $2 per unit per month. What does it cost to hold 500 units for 3 months?
items.why: The real figure is 3000 $. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
